speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is June 26, 1996. The survivor is Marion Gottesman, maiden name Silberberg. The interviewer is Joe Bobrow. We're conducting this interview in Rego Park, New York, in the United States of America, in the English language. Today's date is June 26 1996. The survivor is Marion Gottesman, maiden name Silberberg. My name is Joe Bobrow. We're conducting this interview in the English language in Rego Park, New York, in the United States of America.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Marion Gottesman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my maiden name was [INAUDIBLE] Silberberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell both less names. Your current last name now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My current--

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Gottesman, is G-O-T-T-E-S-M-A-N. And my maiden name was S-I-L-B-E-R-B-E-R-G, Silberberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Vlotho in Northern part of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: V-L-O-T-H-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: What large city is that near?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's between Bielefeld and Hannover.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: So that would make you how old now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- two days ago, I was 69.

speaker: interviewer
text: Happy birthday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a very small town. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember too good of things from-- even from before the war, because I was six years old when I started school in 1933. And we had a very, very tough time, from the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I said before I started school in 193. And a half a year, a year later, we had to sit in a Jew [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: In the where?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a bench only for Jewish children, in front of all the others. And there was a lot of anti-Semitism in the northern part of Germany. So we felt it from the time we were very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remember at the age of six already feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about before six years old? Do you Have any earlier memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: The earlier-- yeah, I have memories from my family, and from my grandmother, which I was very close with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's start talking about-- what was your grandmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother's name was Rebekka Silberberg. And she came from Krefeld, which is in Rhineland, and got married to her own cousin, first cousin, Israel Silberberg, which I don't remember. He died in 1926. And they lived in Vlotho. And my grandmother-- we lived with my grandmother for a while, in the end of-- before we got arrested. And I don't know. It's, I guess, a normal childhood with a few Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about memories of your grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother was a big, huge woman, very lovable, and always feeding us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she a good cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite dish that she made?

speaker: interviewee
text: Offhand, I really don't know. I just know that she was a very good cook. And she always was interested that nobody should get hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you have very close to your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she do things together with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In those times, grandmothers were grandmothers. There weren't people who did things with you outside of the house. Let's put it that way. And she just was a level-- a very lovable person.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by, she was a grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not like here. You become a grandmother in America, you run around and do all the things you do when you're young, right? But over there, it's like-- I guess it was like a normal way of grandparents, or grandmothers at the time, being the motherly types for the whole families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name is Henny, Henny Silberberg. And her maiden name was Strauss.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-R-A-U-S-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a person was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a beautiful woman. She was very compassionate. She always tried to help people. And her profession was a nurse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she work as a nurse outside of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was before she got married that she was a nurse. She was at the university in Giessen, which is near Frankfurt-- yes, nurse there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was a very-- I don't know how to explain to-- he wasn't only my father. He was my friend. He was everything to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was everything to him, that I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Willy, Willy Silberberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Willy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were very close to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very close. I was an only child. I was the daughter he wanted. But I also was the son he wanted. He make me grew up like a tough little kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember doing together with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of bicycle riding. We didn't have a car. So we went from one town to the other, visiting friends and people he had business to do with. And, yes, it was my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he strict?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not really, not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get into trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what would be a typical thing that you would do to get in trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a boy I grew up with in the town, a Jewish boy. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the trouble we got into is that we come from a small little hilly town. And we found, naturally, the highest little mountain there. We would climb up there in dangerous positions, and things like this, and just got-- you know, like little troubles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would your parents do when they found out that you did these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were glad we came home in one piece.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the town itself. What did it look like? How large was it? How many people lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was a child-- now, it became a bigger town, because they incorporated a lot of small towns. It was a small town with 5,000 people living there. And what I remember, there were never more than 20 Jewish families in town. And little by little in the beginning, some people moved, and immigrated to the states, or somewhere else. So by the time that I really remember, there were maybe 10 families left, at the most.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had this one boy. His name was Gunter Katz. And he was survived also Auschwitz, et cetera. And he never came back to Vlotho. He was sent from Vlotho to Holland. From Holland, he came to Theresienstadt, from Theresienstadt to Auschwitz. And when he was liberated, I don't know where he was liberated, the Russians took him to Odessa, and flew him back to Holland. And from in Holland, he found out that none of his family except one aunt survived. And she was in Belgium. So she went to-- he went to Brussels to meet her there, and then came to the United States. But he passed away here.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you met him later on in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You recognized each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he got in touch with us. I don't know how come he got our address. I think he found out through one of the organizations that my mother and I were the only survivors who came back from Vlotho. And I believe that he got in touch with us already in 1946. We came here in '47. So we knew that he was in Brussels. And so we had each other's address. And we knew when he would come here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides this friend, did you have many other friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other Jewish children I was with, nobody survived. We were very close. It was a close little town friendships there. And the first transport went to [INAUDIBLE]. So nobody came back from there. Then a transport went to Riga. They got killed there. And, actually, the only ones who came back to the town was my mother and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you remember having a large group of friends when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were about five, six children there, not more than that. And there were different ages, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived close by to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was a small town, you know. You couldn't help it. You're close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have non-Jewish friends also?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember some non-Jewish friends. But they didn't last long, because even if they wanted to be friendly, they weren't permitted. They couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of games did you play as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: We built our little cars. We didn't have toys like nowadays. So we build just little cars and stuff like that. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you build it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We actually took wood, and put, like-- I don't know what you call those little cars the kid pull and put things on? They have them here too, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, like a wagon. That's what I meant with a car. We did things like that, and got little wheels from somewhere, and et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything-- any other games that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really, I mean, you know, like, I guess what every kid plays. I don't know. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you were active in sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have an opportunity to be active. What I did is with all the boys there, I played soccer as a kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the only girl left, because everybody else had left already. And the others were a little older. They went to different schools out of that town. So, you know, it was always with the boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what kind of a student you were? Were you a good student? Was school work easy for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't the best student. That's for sure. I didn't like school, because, you know, like, we had one teacher who was very nice to us, who actually, himself, got into trouble because of us Jewish children. The other teachers were very bad at that particular time already. And we were glad when we got to school, and got out of school, and they didn't molest us outside, the kids, et cetera. And we were glad to get home. School was, like, not very pleasant those years. All the schooling I have in Germany, really, was from 1933 to 1938. And then after Kristallnacht, nobody could go to school anymore. And then they had, in another town-- but by that time we were only, like, maybe three or four children left, I went into another town to school. And the others went to Holland. And two of the children who went to Holland, matter of fact, they found out from relatives in Holland that their parents were in a transport to Riga. And they went over the back to Germany to go with their parents. So it's, like, not the best times to remember before the war either.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your best subject?

speaker: interviewee
text: My best subject, art.

speaker: interviewer
text: Drawing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think art, drawing, art, and literature, things like that, things I was interested in. It wasn't mathematics. That's for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was just going to ask what was your worst subject?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mathematics. But I believe it has something to do with the teacher we had, because for each subject we had different teachers. And that was the worst Nazi anybody could come across. So I think that had a lot to do with it, that I was afraid of him. And I didn't, you know-- that mathematics was something to be afraid of.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that the Jewish children had to sit on a special bench.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was from the first grade that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was shortly after the school started. I started in '33. So in '33, '34 already, at that particular town.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was the total class that you were in, in the classroom? How many children, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would think about 25, 30 children, maybe less. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were two in our class.

speaker: interviewer
text: So two out of 25, approximately.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had to sit next-- it was you and one other person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was this boy I grew up with, and who I met here again. We were sitting in front of everybody. And it was called a Juden [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you teased? Did they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, we-- my school memories are not the fondest and the nicest, believe me. We couldn't go for gym. We couldn't go for sports, in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were Jews. When they had their gym lessons, we had to either go out of the school and wait outside. Or the town was so little, for one hour, we would go home, and then go back. And the same thing was true in musics too, because they had all the Hitler songs, and et cetera. So we were not-- naturally, not interested, but also not permitted to go for music. Religion they had in school. We had to go out. So it was very little schooling, even in those years already.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that most of the teachers also were not nice to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had one nice teacher.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had one very nice teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that teacher's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't know his first name, you know, Mr. Absmayer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What subject did he teach you?

speaker: interviewee
text: German and geography, I believe, literature, things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would he do that made him nice? How-- you mentioned he got in trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you something. He tried-- when the German children started up with us, so he would take them on the side. He would take our side, leave them alone, this and that. So he got himself really into trouble already.

speaker: interviewer
text: He tried to protect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was very protective of us. I did meet him after the war when I came back to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: You recognized him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew where he was. So I went to see him, you know. And he was naturally very glad that I came back, the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: He remembered you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, yeah. I believe he was a teacher of my father in his last years of school, too, because in Europe it was that one teacher was there for so many years that even the parents went to school with them already, you know, on that order.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other teachers you said that were not so nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would they do that you remember that wasn't nice to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mathematics teacher, not only did he hit us. But he also-- like, when a teacher calls you, let's say to the blackboard, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he wouldn't call us by our name. He would, Jew girl, or Jew boy, et cetera. So, naturally, that didn't leave a very good thing in us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned he hit you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He hit us. He did a lot-- they did a lot of things to us. And I rather don't remember them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he hit the other children also, or only you and this other Jewish boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he hit-- no, some of the children were hit. This was a typical European, I guess, in schools to be hit. But he had it out for Gunter and me. That's for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: He hit you more frequently and maybe harder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely, any excuse, any excuse. He didn't need an excuse to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if the other children were bothering you, would he try to protect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he called us the-- very bad names, and things like that, right in front of the children. They fell right into it, because that's what they were taught in the Hitlerjugend, et cetera. So these are the memories of Vlotho schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- well, everything was in walking distance. But we walked about maybe 20 minutes, 15 to 20 minutes, to go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you go to school with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With Gunter.

speaker: interviewer
text: He stopped by and picked you up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they lived a few blocks away from us. And went together, and his brother, who was a year older, and was in another class. And that was it. His brother didn't survive. He went to Auschwitz and didn't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you walked to school, were you safe? Was anyone bothering you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were bothering us. They were calling us names, and things like that. And even some of the parents of the children did that also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Germany in those times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they physically hurt you in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many times, many times when we were kids. We got beaten up in the streets, and things like that. But it was a way of surviving and living then already.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came back home and told your parents what happened, what would they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were terrible upset, because they couldn't do anything. I remember that, at one time, I got into a fight with three boys on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three against you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three against me. And I was always taller and bigger than Gunter. So when they started in with him, I [INAUDIBLE] at the time too. And from one of the boys, the father was a friend, still, at that particular time, of my father. So he came to-- he took me. He must have seen the whole thing. I was, like seven years, eight years old. He took me and brought me home. But his son was the one who beat me up. So this was like one of those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: How badly were you beaten up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes we got beaten up, bloodied, got blue marks, got away with it. We were little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you always try to fight back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was a fighter, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about Gunter? Did he fight back?

speaker: interviewee
text: He would have been a fighter if he was bigger. He was-- matter of fact, he was born in 1926. So he was a year older than me. And he was so tiny at the time when school started for him, which would have been a year before, that they didn't take him. Not because of that he was Jewish, that he physically was not fully developed as a six-year-old one should be. So that's how come he started school with me. And then he became a very strong man later on, one of those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my parents spoke a little French. They had friends from France who lived, at that particular time, in Germany. But the only thing I really heard was German, no Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: We celebrated the holidays. I'm not from a religious background. But we had a synagogue. And had-- I don't know if it was a cantor or a rabbi. We called him rabbi. And for the holidays, we would go to shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the rabbi's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had Mr. Heller, Max Heller. And he left just before the war and came here to the states. And then later, we had somebody, Mr. Lazarus, I believe was his name, yeah, Lazarus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the temple have a special name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just was a synagogue in Vlotho, Vlotho synagogue. I don't know. If it had a name, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there just one synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes, as a matter of fact, it was quite a big synagogue for a small little place like that. There was so few Jews living there. But in that area, were a lot of resorts. And in those resort areas, just happened not to be many Jewish people living there. So they came for the holidays to Vlotho.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite Jewish holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: My favorite Jewish holiday? Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- because there was wine on the table. But as children, we didn't get any wine. So my mother made some kind of a fruit juice at the time, and told me that that was mine. And, usually, Gunter and his brother came over too. And we had Passover together, and with his parents, naturally.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you thought you were drinking wine?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were thinking that we were drinking wine, and always thought we were [YIDDISH], which we were not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does that mean, [YIDDISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being drunk, that much Jewish I know-- knew then already. I mean, we had Jewish words which we used, I think, I remember, but not that we spoke really Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the town have any parks, or any rivers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had a river. We had a river. And as little children, we would go swimming there. But that later on, we couldn't do that anymore either. So there were not many things we could do.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't allowed to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were not allowed to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Germans, by the town, by the burgomeister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would happen if you went swimming when you weren't supposed to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we'd maybe have gone swimming till 1937, '38. But after that, it was nothing. It was 1936 maybe. It was very hard. It was very, very hard. It was a movie which we could not attend to. There was a big sign up that Jews couldn't-- were not allowed to go in. And I remember my mother taking me to another town to see a movie. And in the middle of one of the movies, it got dark. They turned off the screen, and announced that now that there are Jews in the movie house, and they should leave. So we had to leave in the middle of the performance. That must have been about-- I think it was maybe 1936, '37 already, because after '38, we couldn't even leave the town anymore. So it must have been in 1936, '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you and your mother the only Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were-- we were in a different town. So there were different Jewish people there. So everybody went out. That was way of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't you think about just staying there, pretending-- if you were in a different town, how did they even know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the towns are so small that every-- you know, and so near each other, that everybody knew. They knew. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, I was a child. I did what my mother told me to. I walked out with her. And I remember meeting other people there. And my mother acknowledged them, and they acknowledged us. And then we took, I think, the train back home. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of that movie?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm trying to. I think the English version would be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 'Curly Head,' something like that, a child with curls. And I think the actress was a child actress something, like Shirley-- what was her name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Temple?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shirley Temple was, at the same time-- approximately at the same time, right? [NON-ENGLISH], that I remember. And I think it must have wanted to see that movie, that maybe I read the book, or something like that. That was the movie. I remember. That's the last time I went to the movie there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have a chance to go back to see that movie, the whole movie?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I never did. I never did. One of those.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your house look like, your apartment where you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember three homes. The home where I was born, which was a very huge, large apartment, very modern furniture-- you know, the Bauhaus type what they had in Europe at that time already in the '20s. And we moved from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first address? Do you remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE], 36.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say that again, the name of the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Street, 36.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you moved from there when you were how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been about seven or eight years old, because we had to leave that particular apartment. It wasn't my parents' house. We had an apartment there. And, as a matter of fact, that Gunter, and his mother and father, and his brother, also lived in the same building. And they had to move.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because we were Jews. That started very early there. And we moved to a different area in that small town, like from one end to the other town. And-- but very close to each other. They had, like, an apartment few blocks-- not even few-- a few houses down from where we lived. And it was a smaller apartment. But, you know, when you're a child, you're getting-- you don't know any better. We had our-- still our same furniture and everything. And from there--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live there, this second department?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see, from 1936-37, around that time-- '36 till [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1930-- end of 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about a year, a year and a half?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe a year and a half, two years, something like that. I don't really remember the numbers of anything. And then we moved to my grandmother's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to stop the tape now. But we'll resume in just a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, fine.
